qa_pairs.question: What kind of programming language is C++?
qa_pairs.answer: C++ is a general-purpose, high-level, multi-paradigm programming language.

qa_pairs.question: Who created C++ and in what year?
qa_pairs.answer: C++ was created by Bjarne Stroustrup at Bell Labs in 1979.

qa_pairs.question: How was C++ developed?
qa_pairs.answer: C++ was developed by extending the C programming language.

qa_pairs.question: Name some of the ISO C++ standards that have been released.
qa_pairs.answer: Some ISO C++ standards include C++98, C++03, C++11, C++14, C++17, and C++20.

qa_pairs.question: What is the typical naming convention for ISO C++ standards?
qa_pairs.answer: ISO C++ standards are usually referred to by the year of their release, such as C++98, C++03, C++11, C++14, C++17, and C++20.

qa_pairs.question: How frequently does the ISO C++ committee release new standards?
qa_pairs.answer: The ISO C++ committee releases its standards on a three-year cycle.

qa_pairs.question: When is the next C++ standard expected after C++20?
qa_pairs.answer: After C++20, the next C++ standard is expected in 2023.

qa_pairs.question: Where can one find a definitive, up-to-date, free online wiki reference for C++?
qa_pairs.answer: A definitive, up-to-date, free online wiki reference for C++ (and its C Standard Library subset) is cppreference.com.

qa_pairs.question: Is the C language the same as the C++ language?
qa_pairs.answer: No, the C language is a distinct language from the C++ language.

qa_pairs.question: Can C code be used within C++ projects?
qa_pairs.answer: While a lot of C code can be used as-is in C++, there are times when differences between C and C++ will affect compilation and/or program execution.

qa_pairs.question: How should you refer to your code if you're compiling C code as C++ code and seeking assistance?
qa_pairs.answer: If you are compiling your C code as C++ code, you should refer to it as C++ code when asking for help, as it is being compiled as C++ code.

qa_pairs.question: What was lacking in ISO C++ standards concerning concurrency and memory models prior to 2011?
qa_pairs.answer: Prior to 2011, the ISO C++ standards had no definitions of concurrency and memory models.

qa_pairs.question: What are the consequences of compiling concurrent C++ code using pre-C++11 standards?
qa_pairs.answer: In pre-C++11 compiled code, there are no guarantees concerning the ordering of memory reads and writes under concurrency, which is likely undefined behaviour.

qa_pairs.question: Which C++ standard is preferable for compiling concurrent C++ code?
qa_pairs.answer: It is preferable to compile concurrent C++ code as C++11 code or newer.

qa_pairs.question: What are the benefits of using newer C++ compilers and standards?
qa_pairs.answer: Newer compilers and standards allow for full advantage of optimisations from features like `constexpr`, improve code correctness, and incorporate updates to existing definitions.

qa_pairs.question: What happens if an old compiler or standard is used when newer ones are available for C++ code?
qa_pairs.answer: Using an old compiler and/or an older standard when newer ones can be used will not be able to take advantage of newer compiler abilities and C++ definitions, including improved code correctness and optimisations.

qa_pairs.question: Do all C++ compilers implement language features uniformly?
qa_pairs.answer: No, various compilers implement various language features differently, and compiler releases at times only partially implement a specific ISO C++ standard.

qa_pairs.question: Where can one find information about major C++ compilers and their support for specific language features?
qa_pairs.answer: A wiki page at cppreference.com covers virtually all major C++ compilers and lists the earlier compiler versions implementing specific language features, also providing reference links to each compiler's website.

qa_pairs.question: Do many C++ compilers under Linux provide their own Standard Library implementation?
qa_pairs.answer: Many C++ compilers under Linux do not actually provide their own implementation of the C++ Standard Library; instead, they use one normally installed on the system, typically libstdc++ which is distributed with GCC.

qa_pairs.question: Which C++ Standard Library is commonly used by compilers on Linux systems?
qa_pairs.answer: Typically, libstdc++, which is distributed with GCC, is used by C++ compilers on Linux systems.

qa_pairs.question: Why must administrators configure C++ compilers other than GCC on Compute Canada systems to use a specific version of libstdc++?
qa_pairs.answer: Administrators must configure C++ compilers other than GCC on Compute Canada systems to use a specific version of libstdc++ because several versions of GCC (and therefore libstdc++) are typically installed, and improper settings can lead to issues.

qa_pairs.question: What is a crucial piece of advice for users regarding hard-coding paths to administrator-installed libraries?
qa_pairs.answer: Users should never hard-code paths to administrator-installed libraries in order to compile software.

qa_pairs.question: Where can detailed information about Standard Library components supported in libstdc++ be found?
qa_pairs.answer: The GCC documentation has a section which details which Standard Library components are supported in libstdc++.

qa_pairs.question: For those new to C++ or needing an update, what is a recommended starting point?
qa_pairs.answer: If you are new to C++ or need an update, start by checking out the ISO C++ advocacy site's 'Get Started' page, especially its recommended books.

qa_pairs.question: What is the nature of the `volatile` keyword's meaning in C and C++?
qa_pairs.answer: The `volatile` keyword in C and C++ has very specific meanings, and needing to use it in C/C++ code is a rare event.

qa_pairs.question: Under what specific circumstances might `volatile` be used in high-performance computing code?
qa_pairs.answer: Within high-performance computing code, `volatile` might in rare instances be used to declare a variable to tell the compiler not to optimise away load/store side-effects from/to that variable.

qa_pairs.question: Is `volatile` recommended for synchronisation in C/C++?
qa_pairs.answer: No, `volatile` should never be used for synchronisation; instead, C11/C++11 atomic operations and locks should be used.

qa_pairs.question: Does the meaning of `volatile` remain consistent across different programming languages like C/C++ and Java?
qa_pairs.answer: No, `volatile` in other programming languages, such as Java, is often different from its meaning in C/C++.
